result: The other night, I found myself watching a meteor shower from a balcony that seemed to hover between my daily responsibilities and a childhood sense of possibility. Every streak across the sky felt like chalk lines on a cosmic blackboard, quick lessons on impermanence and spectacle. Instead of making wishes, I decided to take inventory of the sounds around me: a refrigerator humming through the open patio door, a neighbor’s laugh, the rhythmic squeak of my own chair when I shifted. These mundane noises built a quiet counterpoint to the celestial show, grounding me while also nudging me toward reflection. I thought about the way people often search upward when they feel small, forgetting that perspective can also be reclaimed by tuning into the immediate textures of life—the chipped paint on the railing, the rough grain of the wood beneath fingertips, the way cooler air gathers strength in the minutes before midnight. In that moment, luxury meant having time to notice. I wondered how many of my worries were actually overgrown hedges of untrimmed attention, blocking views that were still available. The meteors slowed, or maybe my eyes stopped catching them, but the balcony remained, patient, offering a seat to whomever needed a pause. I promised myself to return even without a celestial event to justify the visit. After all, the best constellations are the unrecorded ones we connect between household noises, human voices, and the unexpected glow lingering after lights go out. I slept brighter for having waited there.
